devanagari: मैत्रेय उवाच । निशम्यात्मभुवा गीतं कारणं शङ्कयोज्झिताः । ततः सर्वे न्यवर्तन्त त्रिदिवाय दिवौकसः ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: maitreya uvāca niśamyātmabhuvā gītaṃ kāraṇaṃ śaṅkayojjhitāḥ tataḥ sarve nyavartanta tridivāya divaukasaḥ

translation:
Maitreya said:
Having heard the cause as explained by the Self-born god (Brahmā), all the gods, with their fear dispelled, returned to Svarga.

devanagari: दितिस्तु भर्तुरादेशाद् अपत्यपरिशङ्किनी । पूर्णे वर्षशते साध्वी पुत्रौ प्रसुषुवे यमौ ॥ २ ॥

translation: On account of her husband’s prediction, Diti was apprehensive of the troubles (to be caused) by her progeny[1]. At the end of hundred years she gave birth to male-twins.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
apatya-pariśaṅkīnī—Afraid of harm to her children by Hari—Padaratnāvalī

devanagari: उत्पाता बहवस्तत्र निपेतुर्जायमानयोः । दिवि भुव्यन्तरिक्षे च लोकस्य उरु भयावहाः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: utpātā bahavastatra nipeturjāyamānayoḥ divi bhuvyantarikṣe ca lokasya uru bhayāvahāḥ

translation: At the time of their birth, there were many evil portents boding terrible calamities to the world, appearing in the heavens, on the earth, and in the sky.

devanagari: सहाचला भुवश्चेलुः दिशः सर्वाः प्रजज्वलुः । स उल्काश्च अशनयः पेतुः केतवश्चार्तिहेतवः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: sahācalā bhuvaśceluḥ diśaḥ sarvāḥ prajajvaluḥ sa ulkāśca aśanayaḥ petuḥ ketavaścārtihetavaḥ
translation: The earth, along with mountains, quaked violently everywhere. All the directions were ablaze. Meteors and thunders showered down. Comets foreboding distress appeared (in the sky).

devanagari: ववौ वायुः सुदुःस्पर्शः फूत्कारानीरयन्मुहुः । उन्मूलयन् नगपतीन् वात्यानीको रजोध्वजः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: vavau vāyuḥ suduḥsparśaḥ phūtkārānīrayanmuhuḥ unmūlayan nagapatīn vātyānīko rajodhvajaḥ

translation: An extremely biting stormy wind frequently roaring (through the sky) blew with its army of whirlwinds, uprooting big trees, and raising its banner of dust (aloft).

devanagari: उद्धसत् तडिदम्भोद घटया नष्टभागणे । व्योम्नि प्रविष्टतमसा न स्म व्यादृश्यते पदम् ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: uddhasat taḍidambhoda ghaṭayā naṣṭabhāgaṇe vyomni praviṣṭatamasā na sma vyādṛśyate padam

translation: When stars (and luminaries) in the sky disappeared, being covered by dark clouds roaring with laughter in the form of lightnings, not a single spot could be seen, due to surging darkness.

devanagari: चुक्रोश विमना वार्धिरुदूर्मिः क्षुभितोदरः । सोदपानाश्च सरितः चुक्षुभुः शुष्कपङ्कजाः ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: cukrośa vimanā vārdhirudūrmiḥ kṣubhitodaraḥ sodapānāśca saritaḥ cukṣubhuḥ śuṣkapaṅkajāḥ

translation: The ocean with its tidal waves and its interior agitated (by acquatic animals), roared as if depressed in spirits. Rivers as well as wells and tanks with lotuses withering there-in, were perturbed.

devanagari: मुहुः परिधयोऽभूवन् सराह्वोः शशिसूर्ययोः । निर्घाता रथनिर्ह्रादा विवरेभ्यः प्रजज्ञिरे ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: muhuḥ paridhayo nirghātā rathanirhrādā vivarebhyaḥ prajajñire

translation: There were frequent misty halos round the Sun and the Moon eclipsed by Rāhu. There were roars of thunders without clouds—deep sound like rattling of chariots came out from the mountain caves.

devanagari: अन्तर्ग्रामेषु मुखतो वमन्त्यो वह्निमुल्बणम् । सृगाल उलूक टङ्कारैः प्रणेदुः अशिवं शिवाः ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: antargrāmeṣu mukhato vamantyo vahnimulbaṇam sṛgāla ulūka ṭaṅkāraiḥ praṇeduḥ aśivaṃ śivāḥ

translation: In villages female jackals vomited terrible fire from their mouths, and howled out ominously, along with cries of he-jackals and hooting of owls.

devanagari: सङ्गीतवद् रोदनवद् उन्नमय्य शिरोधराम् । व्यमुञ्चन् विविधा वाचो ग्रामसिंहाः ततस्ततः ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: saṅgītavad rodanavad unnamayya śirodharām vyamuñcan vividhā vāco grāmasiṃhāḥ tatastataḥ
translation: Here and there dogs gave out different types of barking sounds with their neck raised, as if in singing or crying.

devanagari: खराश्च कर्कशैः क्षत्तः खुरैर्घ्नन्तो धरातलम् । खार्कार रभसा मत्ताः पर्यधावन् वरूथशः ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: kharāśca karkaśaiḥ kṣattaḥ khurairghnanto dharātalam khārkāra rabhasā mattāḥ paryadhāvan varūthaśaḥ

translation: Oh Vidura, herds of maddened donkeys ran about kicking the surface of the earth with their hard hoofs and brayings vehemently.

devanagari: रुदन्तो रासभत्रस्ता नीडाद् उदपतन्खगाः । घोषेऽरण्ये च पशवः शकृन् मूत्रमकुर्वत ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: rudanto rāsabhatrastā nīḍād udapatankhagāḥ ghoṣe

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ rud class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ rud class 2 verb]

translation: Birds being terrified by the donkeys, screamed out and flew out of their nests. Beasts which were in the jungle and in their pens, excreted dung and urine.

devanagari: गावः अत्रसन् असृग्दोहाः तोयदाः पूयवर्षिणः । व्यरुदन् देवलिङ्गानि द्रुमाः पेतुर्विनानिलम् ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: gāvaḥ atrasan asṛgdohāḥ toyadāḥ pūyavarṣiṇaḥ vyarudan devaliṅgāni drumāḥ peturvinānilam
translation: Cows were frightened and blood issued from their udders. The clouds showered pus. The idols of gods shed tears. Trees were uprooted though there was no wind.

devanagari: ग्रहान् पुण्यतमानन्ये भगणांश्चापि दीपिताः । अतिचेरुः वक्रगत्या युयुधुश्च परस्परम् ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: grahān puṇyatamānanye bhagaṇāṃścāpi dīpitāḥ aticeruḥ vakragatyā yuyudhuśca parasparam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ dīp ], [vocative plural from √ dīp ]
[nominative plural from √ dīp ], [vocative plural from √ dīp ], [accusative plural from √ dīp ]

translation: Evil planets (like the Mars) crossed and passed over auspicious planets, and the constellations or stars, and reverting in a crooked course, they fought with each other.

devanagari: दृष्ट्वा अन्यांश्च महोत्पातान् अतत्तत्त्वविदः प्रजाः । ब्रह्मपुत्रान् ऋते भीता मेनिरे विश्वसंप्लवम् ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: dṛṣṭvā anyāṃśca mahotpātān atattattvavidaḥ prajāḥ brahmaputrān ṛte bhītā menire viśvasaṃplavam
translation: Seeing such other terrible omens, people, with the exception of Brahmā’s sons (like Sanat Kumāra), being ignorant of the real implication of these portents, were terrified, and thought that the (time of the) destruction of the universe had come.

devanagari: तौ आदिदैत्यौ सहसा व्यज्यमान आत्मपौरुषौ । ववृधातेऽश्मसारेण कायेन अद्रिपती इव ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: tau ādidaityau sahasā vyajyamāna ātmapauruṣau vavṛdhāte

translation: Those two primitive Daityas, with their bodies hard as rock, grew up fast like big mountains, exhibiting their inborn prowess.

devanagari: दिविस्पृशौ हेमकिरीटकोटिभिः । निरुद्धकाष्ठौ स्फुरदङ्गदाभुजौ । गां कंपयन्तौ चरणैः पदे पदे । कट्या सुकाञ्च्यार्कमतीत्य तस्थतुः ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: divispṛśau hemakirīṭakoṭibhiḥ niruddhakāṣṭhau sphuradaṅgadābhujau gāṃ kaṃpayantau caraṇaiḥ pade pade kaṭyā sukāñcyārkamatītya tasthatuḥ

[nominative dual from √ kamp ], [vocative dual from √ kamp ], [accusative dual from √ kamp ]

translation: They stood touching the heavens with the crest of their gold crowns, embracing all the directions by their arms adorned with brilliant armlets, shaking the earth at every step, surpassing the Sun’s lustre by the brilliance of the girdle round their waist.

devanagari: प्रजापतिर्नाम तयोरकार्षीद् । यः प्राक् स्वदेहाद्यमयोरजायत । तं वै हिरण्यकशिपुं विदुः प्रजा । यं तं हिरण्याक्षमसूत साग्रतः ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: prajāpatirnāma tayorakārṣīd yaḥ prāk svadehādyamayorajāyata taṃ vai hiraṇyakaśipuṃ viduḥ prajā yaṃ taṃ hiraṇyākṣamasūta sāgrataḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect active second plural], [imperfect middle third single]
[present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: The Prajāpati (Kāśyapa) gave them names. Of the twins who was first born of his body was known as Hiraṇyakaśipu and people call (the other) Hiraṇyākṣa, whom she (Diti) gave birth first[2].
purport:
Footnote [2]:
When twins are born, the first child that is born is regarded as the younger, for the elder child gets a position behind it in the womb. This concept underlies the above verse.

devanagari: चक्रे हिरण्यकशिपुः दोर्भ्यां ब्रह्मवरेण च । वशे सपालान् लोकान् त्रीन् अकुतोमृत्युरुद्धतः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: cakre hiraṇyakaśipuḥ dorbhyāṃ brahmavareṇa ca vaśe sapālān lokān trīn akutomṛtyuruddhataḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]

[perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]
[perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]

translation: Hiraṇyakaśipu had no fear of death by the boon of Brahmā. He became arrogant. He subdued by the power of his arms, the worlds along with their protectors.

devanagari: हिरण्याक्षो अनुजस्तस्य प्रियः प्रीतिकृदन्वहम् । गदापाणिर्दिवं यातो युयुत्सुः मृगयन् रणम् ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: hiraṇyākṣo anujastasya priyaḥ prītikṛdanvaham gadāpāṇirdivaṃ yāto yuyutsuḥ mṛgayan raṇam
translation: He loved his younger brother Hiraṇyākṣa who wished to please him (Hiraṇyakaśipu). Spoiling for fight every day, he took a mace in his hand, and went to heaven seeking war.

devanagari: तं वीक्ष्य दुःसहजवं रणत् काञ्चन नूपुरम् । वैजयन्त्या स्रजा जुष्टं अंस न्यस्त महागदम् ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ vīkṣya duḥsahajavaṃ raṇat kāñcana nūpuram vaijayantyā srajā juṣṭaṃ aṃsa nyasta mahāgadam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ raṇ class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ raṇ class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ raṇ class 1 verb]

translation: When gods saw him advancing with irresistible speed, making the tinkling sound of his gold anklets, and wearing the Vaijayantī garland[3], shouldering his big mace, proudly confident of his physical and mental powers, and of the boons (conferred by Brahmā), unconfrontable and undeterred, they concealed th emselves like serpents afraid of Garuḍa.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Vaijayantī—(i) A long necklace or garland of eight kinds of pearls—Bhāvārtha-dīpikā-prakāśa
(ii) A four-fold garland of flowers—Padaratnāvalī

devanagari: मनोवीर्यवर उत्सिक्तं असृण्यं अकुतोभयम् । भीता निलिल्यिरे देवाः तार्क्ष्य त्रस्तः इवाहयः ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: manovīryavara utsiktaṃ asṛṇyaṃ akutobhayam bhītā nililyire devāḥ tārkṣya trastaḥ ivāhayaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ tras class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ tras class 4 verb]

translation: When gods saw him advancing with irresistible speed, making the tinkling sound of his gold anklets, and wearing the Vaijayantī garland[3], shouldering his big mace, proudly confident of his physical and mental powers, and of the boons (conferred by Brahmā), unconfrontable and undeterred, they concealed th emselves like serpents afraid of Garuḍa.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Vaijayantī—(i) A long necklace or garland of eight kinds of pearls—Bhāvārtha-dīpikā-prakāśa
(ii) A four-fold garland of flowers—Padaratnāvalī

devanagari: स वै तिरोहितान् दृष्ट्वा महसा स्वेन दैत्यराट् । सइन्द्रान् देवगणान् क्षीबान् अपश्यन् व्यनदद्‍भृशम् ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: sa vai tirohitān dṛṣṭvā mahasā svena daityarāṭ saindrān devagaṇān kṣībān apaśyan vyanadad‍bhṛśam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: When the king of Daityas saw that Indra along with all the gods had verily hid himself at the sight of his terrible might, and was not excited[4], he roared loudly.
purport:
Footnote [4]:
v.l. klībān—the effeminate Indra and the gods etc.

devanagari: ततो निवृत्तः क्रीडिष्यन् गम्भीरं भीमनिस्वनम् । विजगाहे महासत्त्वो वार्धिं मत्त इव द्विपः ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: tato nivṛttaḥ krīḍiṣyan gambhīraṃ bhīmanisvanam vijagāhe mahāsattvo vārdhiṃ matta iva dvipaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ krīḍ class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ krīḍ class 1 verb]
[nominative single from √ mad class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ mad class 3 verb], [nominative single from √ mad class 4 verb]

translation: Returning thence (from Svarga) and with a desire to sport, (Hiraṇyākṣa) of immense might,dived into the unfathomable, terribly roaring ocean, like an intoxicated elephant.

devanagari: तस्मिन्प्रविष्टे वरुणस्य सैनिका । यादोगणाः सन्नधियः ससाध्वसाः । अहन्यमाना अपि तस्य वर्चसा । प्रधर्षिता दूरतरं प्रदुद्रुवुः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: tasminpraviṣṭe varuṇasya sainikā yādogaṇāḥ sannadhiyaḥ sasādhvasāḥ ahanyamānā api tasya varcasā pradharṣitā dūrataraṃ pradudruvuḥ
translation: When he entered the ocean, the soldiers of Varuṇa, viz. all the acquatic animals, lost their morale. Though they were not (physically) struck down (by him), they got terrified, and overwhelmed by his splendour, they ran away to a long distance.

devanagari: स वर्षपूगान् उदधौ महाबलः । चरन् महोर्मीन् श्वसनेरितान्मुहुः । मौर्व्याभिजघ्ने गदया विभावरीं । आसेदिवान् तात पुरीं प्रचेतसः ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: sa varṣapūgān udadhau mahābalaḥ caran mahormīn śvasaneritānmuhuḥ maurvyābhijaghne gadayā vibhāvarīṃ āsedivān tāta purīṃ pracetasaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]

translation: Oh child, for many years he (Hiraṇyākṣa) of monstrous strength, wandered through the ocean often beating down with his mace of black iron[5], huge waves which were frequently swelled by his heavy breath. (Finally) he arrived at Vi- bhāvarī, the capital of Varuṇa.
purport:
Footnote [5]:
Or the mace which was firmly tied down with the rope of mūrvā grass—Bhāvāratha Dīpikā

devanagari: तत्रोपलभ्यासुरलोकपालकं । यादोगणानां ऋषभं प्रचेतसम् । स्मयन् प्रलब्धुं प्रणिपत्य नीचवत् । जगाद मे देह्यधिराज संयुगम् ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: tatropalabhyāsuralokapālakaṃ yādogaṇānāṃ ṛṣabhaṃ pracetasam smayan pralabdhuṃ praṇipatya nīcavat jagāda me dehyadhirāja saṃyugam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: Seeing there Varuṇa, the protector of the Asura region, and the lord of acquatic animals, he smiled, and ridiculing him with a bow like a mean person, he spoke, ‘Your imperial majesty, be pleased to give me a fight’.

devanagari: त्वं लोकपालोऽधिपतिर्बृहच्छ्रवा । वीर्यापहो दुर्मदवीरमानिनाम् । विजित्य लोकेऽखिलदैत्यदानवान् । यद् राजसूयेन पुरायजत्प्रभो ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: tvaṃ lokapālo vīryāpaho durmadavīramāninām vijitya loke yad rājasūyena purāyajatprabho

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ bṛh ], [vocative single from √ bṛh ], [accusative single from √ bṛh ]
[nominative single from √ bṛh ], [vocative single from √ bṛh ], [accusative single from √ bṛh ]
[nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: ‘Oh Lord, you are the protector of the world, a great sovereign of wide reputation. You are the subduer of the valour of warriors who consider themselves haughty and valiant. You have formerly performed the Rājasūya sacrifice after conquering all the Daityas and Dānavas in the world.’

devanagari: स एवं उत्सिक्त मदेन विद्विषा । दृढं प्रलब्धो भगवान् अपां पतिः । रोषं समुत्थं शमयन् स्वया धिया । व्यवोचदङ्गोपशमं गता वयम् ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: sa evaṃ utsikta madena vidviṣā dṛḍhaṃ pralabdho bhagavān apāṃ patiḥ roṣaṃ samutthaṃ śamayan svayā dhiyā vyavocadaṅgopaśamaṃ gatā vayam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect active first single], [aorist active first single]
[nominative single from √ śam ], [vocative single from √ śam ]

translation: The glorious lord of waters who was thus bitterly derided by the enemy who was puffed up with excessive arrogance, controlled, by force of his reason, his anger which was thus provoked. He spoke out, ‘Oh (valiant warrior), we have now grown tranquil.

devanagari: पश्यामि नान्यं पुरुषात् पुरातनाद् । यः संयुगे त्वां रणमार्गकोविदम् । आराधयिष्यति असुरर्षभेहि तं । मनस्विनो यं गृणते भवादृशाः ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: paśyāmi nānyaṃ puruṣāt purātanād yaḥ saṃyuge tvāṃ raṇamārgakovidam ārādhayiṣyati asurarṣabhehi taṃ manasvino yaṃ gṛṇate bhavādṛśāḥ
translation: Oh leader of Asuras, I do not see any other person except the Primeval Man who can satisfy in fight a pastmaster in the science of war like you. Go to him whom high-minded warriors like you eulogize.

devanagari: तं वीरमारादभिपद्य विस्मयः । शयिष्यसे वीरशये श्वभिर्वृतः । यस्त्वद्विधानां असतां प्रशान्तये । रूपाणि धत्ते सदनुग्रहेच्छया ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ vīramārādabhipadya vismayaḥ śayiṣyase vīraśaye śvabhirvṛtaḥ yastvadvidhānāṃ asatāṃ praśāntaye rūpāṇi dhatte sadanugrahecchayā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ vṛ class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ vṛ class 5 verb], [nominative single from √ vṛ class 9 verb]
[nominative single from √ vṛ class 5 verb], [nominative single from √ vṛ class 9 verb]

translation:
He takes incarnations for putting down wicked persons like you, and for favouring the good, with his grace. You approach that warrior. You will (then) be rid of your pride and will lie (slain) in the bed of warriors (battlefield) surrounded by dogs.”
